turns.content: I'm working through the book exercises on traits and I have a solution that defines an associated const in my trait impl. I used the syntax to define it as type i32 with value 42 inside the implementation block for MyStruct, but I noticed some comments in the book suggest using const generics instead. Is there a better approach here?

For defining a fixed constant that doesn't depend on any generic parameters, an associated constant is definitely the right choice rather than a const generic. The key distinction is that const generics like T or N are placeholders for compile-time values that can vary between different implementations of a trait, whereas associated constants are fixed values specific to that particular type's implementation. Since your value 42 doesn't change regardless of what other parameters you might pass later, using an associated constant keeps the API simpler.

That makes sense for varying values, but I'm confused about when they actually matter. Can't I just declare a const i32 = 42 at the top level and use that everywhere instead of putting it in the impl block?

You can define a regular module-level constant like std::os::raw::c_int or similar, but those are not associated constants. The difference is crucial because only types that implement a trait with an associated const field can access it through the type itself without knowing the concrete implementation. If you move 42 to the top level as a plain const i32 = 42, then code using MyStruct won't be able to refer to that value as if it were part of the type's interface.
So I can't just hoist it out? That seems like a lot of boilerplate for just one number. Is there really no way to keep it scoped without repeating the definition everywhere?

The reason is that Rust's type system needs to know this value exists as part of the trait contract before any concrete implementation happens. When you have a generic trait with an associated const, every implementing type must provide it so downstream code can call methods or access properties without needing to know which specific struct was used. This is why the definition lives in the impl block and not at module scope.
I see now, but what if I want different implementations of MyTrait to have different constants? Should I use const generics then?

Yes, that's exactly when you'd switch to a const generic parameter. If your trait needs multiple implementations with distinct constant values like 42 for one type and 100 for another, then defining the associated const as SomeType or N in the trait definition allows each implementation to specify its own value during the impl block while maintaining consistency across all uses of the trait.

Got it. So the rule is: fixed same-value constants go in impl blocks as associated consts, and variable per-implement values use const generics. One last question about syntax—do I need to add a space before the equals sign like const VALUE = 42 or can it be constVALUE=42?

The correct syntax requires a space: const NAME = value. Writing constNAME=value without spaces is invalid Rust and will cause a parse error because the compiler expects whitespace around the assignment operator in this context.
